summary.brief: Debra Chesnut v. United States. Medical malpractice case involving neurology.

evidence_index.text: On April 4, 2016, Debra went to the emergency room at Manchester Memorial Hospital in Manchester, Kentucky, where she presented with numbness from the waist down, pain in both legs, with the right leg being worse and cold.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: On April 19, 2016, Debra’s right leg was amputated below the knee at the University of Kentucky Medical Center (“UKMC”) due to serious clots that restricted blood flow.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Dr. Madden then proceeded to his examination. He found that Debra’s dorsalis pedis pulse, from an artery in the foot, which is a measure of blood flow to the legs, was normal.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: The district court found that Dr. Madden had been negligent when he had examined Debra on April 12, 2016, and failed to meet the applicable standard of care when he did not consider vascular causes of Debra’s symptoms in diagnosing her.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: The district court found in favor of the United States on Plaintiffs’ claims because it determined that Plaintiffs had “failed to prove by way of expert testimony that Dr. Madden’s deviation from the standard of care was a proximate cause of Ms. Chesnut’s below-the-knee amputation.”
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Dr. Roberts explained that the amputation of Debra’s leg became necessary after seeing Dr. Madden because “legs don’t really become cyanotic until you knock off the profunda. And the profunda is up in the thigh.”
evidence_index.speaker: Dr. Roberts

evidence_index.text: Dr. Roberts opined that the clotting in Debra’s leg observed at the University of Kentucky on April 13 would have taken “on average . . . five to seven days.”
evidence_index.speaker: Dr. Roberts

evidence_index.text: The district court’s factual findings as to causation were predicated on multiple misunderstandings of Kentucky law, internally inconsistent analysis, and implausible factual findings.
evidence_index.speaker: Court reasoning

evidence_index.text: For the reasons stated above, we REVERSE the district court’s decision and REMAND for proceedings consistent with this opinion.
evidence_index.speaker: Court conclusion

initial_state.chief_complaint: Pain and numbness in legs, right leg worse and cold
initial_state.history_of_present_illness: Sudden onset of pain and numbness in legs on April 4, 2016. Right leg colder than left.

focused_exam.legs: Right leg cool to touch, left leg warm, pulses present in both legs

requestables.name: CT Angiogram
requestables.description: Imaging to assess blood flow and identify clots

reveal.result_summary: Serious blood clots in right leg

timeline_phases.name: Initial Presentation
timeline_phases.description: Patient presents to emergency room with leg symptoms

key_events.event: Patient seen by Dr. Thomas and diagnosed with sciatica

timeline_phases.name: Follow-up and Examination
timeline_phases.description: Patient seen by APRN Cole and Dr. Madden

key_events.event: Patient seen by APRN Cole, diagnosed with sciatica
Patient seen by Dr. Madden, diagnosed with musculoskeletal issues

timeline_phases.name: Emergency Diagnosis
timeline_phases.description: Patient experiences worsening symptoms and is diagnosed with ischemia

key_events.event: Patient's right foot turns blue, diagnosed with ischemia

decision_points.clinical_context: Patient with leg pain and numbness, right leg colder than left
decision_points.prompt: What should be included in the differential diagnosis?

options.description: Include vascular causes in differential diagnosis
options.clinical_reasoning: Standard of care requires considering vascular issues given symptoms

expected_action_court.description: Include vascular causes in differential diagnosis
expected_action_court.standard_of_care_basis: Expert testimony indicated vascular issues should have been considered

explanation.why_defendant_wrong: Failed to consider vascular causes despite symptoms
explanation.what_should_have_happened: Vascular causes should have been included in differential diagnosis
explanation.legal_standard_applied: Standard of care as established by expert testimony

patient_outcome.description: Right leg amputation below the knee
patient_outcome.long_term_effects: Loss of limb, need for prosthesis

key_findings: District court found no liability due to causation issues
Appellate court reversed and remanded for further proceedings

malpractice_determination.point_of_failure: Failure to refer to vascular surgery
malpractice_determination.counterfactual: Timely referral could have prevented amputation

factual_timeline.event: Patient presents to emergency room with leg pain and numbness
Patient seen by Dr. Madden, diagnosed with musculoskeletal issues
Patient diagnosed with ischemia at emergency room
Patient arrives at ED with leg symptoms
Right leg amputation at University of Kentucky

tests_performed.result: Serious blood clots in right leg

allegations.allegation: Failure to refer to vascular surgery

court_findings.finding: Dr. Madden was negligent but not a substantial factor in causing amputation
District court found no liability due to causation issues
